title: The Precarious State of America's Treasury Market
source: The Guardian - The Economist  WEB  06 -12  June 2026
content:
Since Richard Nixon broke the link between the American dollar and gold in 1971, global finance has essentially floated on a sea of U.S. Treasuries. Historically, no asset has been more important than America’s government debt, as it provides a safe haven for investors during dangerous economic moments. Trillions of dollars of contracts and securities worldwide are priced with direct reference to these Treasury bonds. Alas, the world’s safest asset has undeniably seen better days, and its foundational stability is increasingly coming into question. The total volume of outstanding Treasuries has grown by an astonishing 126% over the past decade, reaching almost $32 trillion.

This massive growth has far outstripped steady demand from traditional buyers like foreign central banks. As a result, yield-hunting private investors and highly leveraged hedge funds have taken a rapidly growing share of the market. Occasionally, most notably during the market shock of March 2020, this private demand has suddenly dried up, sending short-term funding costs surging. These liquidity crises frequently force the Federal Reserve to step in, buy bonds, and effectively underwrite the entire market to prevent collapse. Furthermore, broader economic and geopolitical trends are actively hurting long-term international demand for U.S. Treasuries.

The resurgence of global inflation since 2021 has often caused stocks and government bonds to sell off simultaneously. Consequently, Treasuries have largely ceased to play their traditional, valuable role as reliable ballast for riskier investment portfolios. Meanwhile, America’s increasingly belligerent trade policies and its repeated deployment of financial sanctions have made foreign buyers hesitant to remain long-term creditors to Uncle Sam. If these troubling patterns continue, the Treasury market risks losing its special global status, which currently provides crucial economic benefits to both America and the wider world. Salvaging this precarious situation raises tough strategic questions for the new chair of the Federal Reserve and the Treasury Secretary.

The Federal Reserve aims to reduce its massive holdings of Treasuries, which is an achievable goal but requires significantly more private demand. Concurrently, the Fed must always stand ready to intervene and buy bonds to calm sudden market seizures. Any future intervention will require a much defter hand to avoid confusing short-lived "market functioning" interventions with broad economic stimulus. It would be disastrous if emergency bond purchases to unclog financial plumbing were widely interpreted as bowing to political pressure for loose money. Reforming the underlying market structure will also require America’s various financial regulators to work in closer tandem with the Federal Reserve.

Perhaps the most important remedy for the decaying Treasury market is in the hands of politicians who control fiscal policy. The size of America’s current budget deficit, at around 6% of GDP, has no parallels during peacetime except during very deep recessions. Further fiscal pressure is looming, particularly as the Social Security trust fund faces exhaustion in the coming years. Both the executive branch and the legislature must summon the political will to confront the underlying long-term problem and significantly shrink federal borrowing. Stopping the steady erosion of the Treasury market is critical, as America’s role as the global supplier of safe assets saves the country billions annually.
